segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: Listen to a talk in a psychology class.

segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: Cognitive dissonance is a term that describes the mental discomfort a person experiences due to contradictory behaviors or beliefs.

segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: The concept was introduced by psychologist Leon Festinger in 1957.

segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: According to Festinger, people are motivated to reduce this discomfort by changing their beliefs or behaviors.

segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: This motivation can be used in product marketing.

segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: If a consumer is worried about the environmental impact of gasoline cars but still drives one, this could cause cognitive dissonance.

segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: A marketing campaign for electric vehicles could take advantage of this by highlighting the harm done by gasoline cars and so increase the dissonance.

segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: Then electric cars can be implicitly touted as a way of resolving the mental conflict.

segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: Cognitive dissonance can also be useful in education because it can encourage critical thinking.

segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: Say that a student believes that the American Revolution in the 1700s was driven only by a desire for independence from British rule.

segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: Then a teacher might present evidence that economic factors, like taxation and trade restrictions, also played a role.

segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: The resulting mental discomfort might stimulate the student to start questioning their initial belief and to seek more information on the subject.

segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: Understanding cognitive dissonance can help us promote positive behavioral change.
